title: Makroud
subtitle: Moroccan semolina pastries filled with spiced dates and dipped in honey
excerpt: Makroud are diamond-shaped Moroccan semolina pastries with a date and cinnamon filling. The semolina dough is rolled, filled, sealed and cut diagonally into diamonds, then deep-fried until golden and immediately dipped in warm honey. The semolina gives the pastry a sandy, tender crumb that absorbs the honey without becoming soggy. They keep well for several days in an airtight tin. The total time is about 1 hour 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: Makroud are common across Morocco, Tunisia and Algeria, with regional variations in filling and finishing. Anissa Helou documents them in 'Feast: Food of the Islamic World' (2018) and Paula Wolfert in 'Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco' (1973). The diagonal cut that gives the diamond shape is consistent across all primary sources. The Tunisian version is often baked rather than fried; the Moroccan version is almost always fried.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before dipping in honey. Defrost fully and warm briefly, then dip in warm honey before serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Makroud keep well in an airtight tin for up to 5 days at room temperature. The honey coating softens them slightly over time, which many people prefer.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: deep-fried at 170 to 175 degrees C; no oven required

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique that produces the characteristic diamond shape. The semolina dough is rolled into a flat rectangle, the date filling is laid along the centre as a log, and the dough is folded up around it and sealed by pressing the edges together. The filled log is then cut at a 45-degree diagonal at even intervals of about 3 cm, producing diamond-shaped pieces. The diagonal cut is not decorative: it exposes the filling at each cut face, giving the honey glaze a surface to adhere to, and means each piece has two pointed ends and two flat faces.

content.text: The semolina dough should be just firm enough to roll and seal around the filling without cracking. If it crumbles when pressed, add the olive oil a little more slowly and work it in thoroughly.
is made in one confident stroke with a sharp knife; sawing tears the dough at the seam.
Dip the fried makroud in honey immediately on leaving the oil, while both are still hot. The honey glaze soaks in slightly as the pastry cools; once set, it forms a thin, slightly sticky coat.

Mash the 250 g stoned dates with the 30 g butter, 1 tsp cinnamon and 1 tbsp orange blossom water until smooth and paste-like. Divide into four equal portions and roll each into a log about 1.5 cm in diameter and as long as your dough strips will be. Set aside.

Mix the 300 g semolina, 50 g flour and 0.5 tsp salt. Pour in the 80 g melted butter and 2 tbsp olive oil and rub into the semolina until the mixture resembles damp sand. Add the 60 ml warm water gradually and mix until the dough comes together into a firm, pliable ball that does not crack when pressed. Rest for 15 minutes, covered.

Divide the dough into four. Roll one piece into a flat strip about 6 cm wide and 3 to 4 mm thick. Lay one date log along the centre. Fold both long edges of the dough up over the filling and press firmly to seal. Flatten the sealed log lightly with a rolling pin to about 2 cm high and 3 cm wide. Cut at a 45-degree diagonal at 3 cm intervals to make diamond shapes. Repeat with the remaining dough and filling. Heat the 1 litre oil to 170 to 175 degrees C. Fry in batches for 3 to 4 minutes each side until deep golden brown. Lift out and immediately dip in the warm honey. Drain on a rack.

items.problem: Dough cracked when folding around the filling
items.solution: The dough was too dry. Add the warm water a teaspoon at a time and work it in thoroughly before adding more. The dough should be firm but pliable, not crumbly.

items.problem: Seam split open during frying and filling leaked out
items.solution: The seam was not pressed firmly enough. When sealing the dough over the filling, press hard along the full length. A short rest of 5 minutes before frying helps the seam hold.

items.problem: Honey glaze did not adhere and slid off
items.solution: The pastry had cooled before dipping. Both the makroud and the honey should be hot at the moment of dipping. Keep the honey warm in a small pan over low heat throughout the frying.
